nodes.label: Neurotoxicity Following Exposure to Micro and Nanoplastics

data.title: Neurotoxicity Following Exposure to Micro and Nanoplastics
data.exposure_context: neurotoxic implications of nanoplastics across various species and in vitro settings
data.notes: The review includes studies published between 2015 and 2024.

data.identification_capability: confirmed absorption and presence of particles in freeze-dried tissues

data.label: oxidative_stress
data.notes: The paper explicitly reports oxidative stress or oxidative damage in multiple summarized studies.

data.label: inflammation
data.notes: The paper reports upregulated TNF-α and IL-6 in mouse brain tissues exposed to PS-NH2.

data.label: metabolic_disturbance
data.notes: The paper reports dose-dependent alterations in energy metabolism in mouse liver.

data.label: genotoxicity
data.notes: The paper explicitly states that polystyrene microbeads did not cause genotoxic effects in Dreissena polymorpha.

data.label: behavioral changes
data.notes: The abstract states that exposure could inhibit acetylcholinesterase activity and alter neurotransmitter levels, potentially contributing to observed behavioral changes.

data.label: neurological disorders
data.notes: The abstract states that exposure to nanoplastics may lead to oxidative stress, potentially causing cell damage and raising the risk of developing neurological disorders.

nodes.label: lowered survival rates and decreased average lifespan

data.label: lowered survival rates and decreased average lifespan
data.notes: Caenorhabditis elegans exposed to spherical polystyrene microplastics showed lowered survival rates and decreased average lifespan.
During the initial week of exposure, 5 μm polystyrene particle concentrations stabilized at approximately 0.2 mg/g in liver.
During the initial week of exposure, 5 μm polystyrene particle concentrations stabilized at approximately 1.0 mg/g in kidney.
During the initial week of exposure, 5 μm polystyrene particle concentrations stabilized at approximately 1.4 mg/g in gut.
For 20 μm particles, the concentration was more consistent across the organs, reaching a plateau at about 0.8.
Polyethylene particle uptake was confirmed after 14-28 days at 1.5 g/kg soil.

data.value: accumulation of particles within the gut lumen and further movement to tissues and hemolymph
data.notes: Dreissena polymorpha exposed to pristine polystyrene microbeads of 1 μm and 10 μm at concentrations of 1 and 4 × 106 MPs/L for six days.

data.value: particles detected in the hemolymph, digestive gland, and gills
data.notes: Scrobicularia plana exposed to 20 μm polystyrene microplastics at a concentration of 1 mg/L.

data.value: plastic particles within the hemolymph, gills, and intestines
data.notes: Mytilus galloprovincialis exposed to unpolluted and pyrene-contaminated polyethylene and polystyrene microplastics (100 μm, 1.5 g/L) over seven days.

data.value: plastic particles inside the digestive tract, gland's lumen, connective tissue, hemolymphatic sinuses, and the surface of the gills
data.notes: Corbicula flumina exposed to red fluorescent polymeric microspheres (composition unspecified; 1-5 μm, 0.2 or 0.7 mg/L) for 96 hours.
Amphibia lanu amphitrite and Artemia Franciscan larvae exposed to 0.1 μm fluorescent polystyrene microparticles at 0.001 to 10 mg/L over 24 to 48 hours.
Mouse brain tissues exposed to PS-NH2 showed increased caspase-3 signals.
Mouse brain tissues exposed to PS-NH2 showed TNF-α and IL-6 upregulation.
Polystyrene microbeads did not cause genotoxic effects in Dreissena polymorpha.

data.study_scope: nanoplastics across various species and in vitro settings
data.notes: The abstract states: current evidence, although incomplete, suggests that exposure to nanoplastics may lead to oxidative stress.
The abstract states that exposure could inhibit acetylcholinesterase activity and alter neurotransmitter levels, potentially contributing to observed behavioral changes.

Caenorhabditis elegans exposed to five different spherical polystyrene microplastic sizes (0.1-5 μm) at 1 mg/L showed movement effects, lowered survival rates, and decreased average lifespan; the paper notes no direct proof of ingestion.
The paper states that expression of several neuronal genes declined, which was linked to disruptions in cholinergic and GABA neurons and increased oxidative stress.

Male rats chronically exposed over five weeks to 40 nm polystyrene nanoplastics at 1 to 10 mg/kg body weight per day showed no impact on behavior or weight gain, and no evidence was provided of actual uptake.
The paper states that polystyrene microbeads did not cause genotoxic effects in Dreissena polymorpha.
